kept.text:
I’m really struggling to see how the back of the envelope math on this works out…

There are generously 4 million characterized “software workers” in America. That’s pretty broad and includes a lot of people who aren’t really classical engineers don’t produce that much code. 

That comes out to nearly $1k per month of average Claude spend across every dev in America. Yes, there’s some international usage, but it can’t be that much. Yes there is some non software Cowork usage, but that doesn’t use that many tokens. Yes, some non engineers are using Claude to vibe code, but I really doubt many are spending hundreds per month on. 

Even if we assume 50% of all software workers are using Claude, that comes out to $2k spend per month per Claude user. Thats 10X more than the highest tier Max subscription. So almost all of Anthropics revenue has to be API billing 

So the only explanation is that something like 20%+ of software engineers are not only Claude users but on API billing and regularly spending thousands per month. At $5/m Opus tokens that means the average API user has to be going through something like 25 million tokens per day.

*OR* the other possibility is API revenue is heavily power law dominated. Maybe there’s just something like 100k super users who are making up the majority of the revenue. For that to work the typical super user would have to be spending on the order of $50k/month and guzzling nearly 1 billion tokens per day.

kept.text:
Commentary:

Saw a sharp post framing my Reddit position as a closed loop: Claude buys Reddit, Reddit hosts users asking Claude questions, Claude monetizes its own users. It's a clean joke. The actual mechanism runs differently. The loop is open.

Reddit licenses its conversation graph to every frontier model that wants to train on it. Anthropic, OpenAI, Google all pay separately. "Other revenue" ran $39M in Q1 at roughly 95% margins, annualizing to about $155M, and sell-side currently treats that line as a kicker. The actual call is whether the 2027 renewal cycle takes it from kicker to core driver. Each incremental $100M of AI-licensing revenue at 95% margins is roughly $3 to $5 per share at consensus multiples. If two of three big licensees renew at higher rates and a fourth signs (Meta, xAI, take your pick), the math compounds.

RDDT is roughly 5.6% of my book after Q1 print follow-through took the stock from a $135 entry to $170. My probability-weighted 12M target is $189: bull $245, base $185, bear $115. The position is sized for "AI-licensing TAM is bigger than consensus" rather than for the next print. Kill condition is logged-in DAU rolling negative for two consecutive quarters or a major licensee renewing flat or down. You're watching the experiment, not getting a tip.

I’m really struggling to see how the back of the envelope math on this works out…

There are generously 4 million characterized “software workers” in America. That’s pretty broad and includes a lot of people who aren’t really classical engineers don’t produce that much code. 

That comes out to nearly $1k per month of average Claude spend across every dev in America. Yes, there’s some international usage, but it can’t be that much. Yes there is some non software Cowork usage, but that doesn’t use that many tokens. Yes, some non engineers are using Claude to vibe code, but I really doubt many are spending hundreds per month on. 

Even if we assume 50% of all software workers are using Claude, that comes out to $2k spend per month per Claude user. Thats 10X more than the highest tier Max subscription. So almost all of Anthropics revenue has to be API billing 

So the only explanation is that something like 20%+ of software engineers are not only Claude users but on API billing and regularly spending thousands per month. At $5/m Opus tokens that means the average API user has to be going through something like 25 million tokens per day.

*OR* the other possibility is API revenue is heavily power law dominated. Maybe there’s just something like 100k super users who are making up the majority of the revenue. For that to work the typical super user would have to be spending on the order of $50k/month and guzzling nearly 1 billion tokens per day.

@c_pick TBH I don't agree with your take. I don't think Athropic's desire to control the harness is about keeping resource usage under control. They could accomplish that by just enforcing limits on the actual resource usage (which they already do) -- if some third-party harness is inefficient, users of than harness hit their limits faster.

I think instead that they want to control the harness because if switching LLM providers is too easy, it makes business difficult for the providers. Say GPT 5.5 comes out and it's clearly smarter, faster, and cheaper than Opus 4.7. If everyone can switch providers with two clicks in their harness, many of them will. This would lead to wild revenue and usage swings, which makes capacity planning hard. And perfect competition drives down prices -- in this scenario Opus has to cut its prices to get some users back.

Obviously no business wants to be in that situation!

By controlling the harness, they add some stickiness. If switching LLM providers means switching harnesses, that's a barrier high enough that most people won't bother to do it on a whim. So now Opus 4.7 can weather the storm until 4.8 or whatever comes out and is back on top.

So it makes perfect sense to me as a business decision. It may be user-unfriendly, but tech companies do stuff like this all the time. It's nothing new.

Though I would say, it seems weird to me to do this *on top of* subscriptions. Subscriptions already create a lot of stickiness. If you're subscribed only to Claude, that's a pretty big barrier to trying out GPT quickly -- a bigger barrier than the harness barrier I think. So I question whether controlling the harness is really worth all the effort they are putting into it, but idk, they probably have insights that I don't on this.

Another factor here might actually be safety concerns. As we know, Anthropic leadership is deeply (excessively, IMO) worried about AI safety, and they feel that Anthropic will do a better job of addressing safety than any other company. They may feel that control of the harness is an important tool for that. I could definitely imagine Dario being terrified of OpenClaw from a safety perspective (I sort of am too).

These explanations make much more sense to me than the efficiency issue, which again seems like it could easily be managed in other ways. But of course, these explanations are much harder to just come out and say, without stirring a lot more outrage...

kept.text:
Claude Codeの導入検討してる企業さんからの問い合わせが増えているんだけど、よく話す内容をまとめてみる。

Claude Codeの使い方を学ぶのは大事だけど、それ以上にユーザー(従業員)のことを信じすぎないことが大事。セキュリティやリスク管理を従業員任せにすると事故る可能性が高くなる。

「APIキーをチャットに貼ったらダメ」と言ってても余裕でチャットに貼る。「機密情報は書くな」といくら言っても機密情報が何か分からずチャットに書く。人間だから、うっかりミスしてしまうこともあり得る。

使う人数が増えれば増えるほどにリスクは高くなって、どこかのタイミングで情報漏洩して大事故につながる、なんてのは企業として絶対に避けたいはず。

重要なことは、そういうのを理解した上で安全に使えるガードレールの仕組みを会社として用意しておくこと。

要するにAPIキーや機密情報を間違って送信しても、それがAnthropicなどのサーバーに送信される前に止めてやれば大事には至らない(全てを止めることはできないが)。

あとは各従業員の使用状況やどんなやりとりしているかを定期的にモニタリング、分析して改善していく仕組みもある方がいい。定着しないと意味はないし、危険な使い方をしている人がいれば、それは指導をする必要もある。

kept.text:
🔎Anthropic、ARR440億ドル超・推論マージン70%超 | 価値獲得はモデル側へ移る $NVDA

AIプラットフォームの収益化をめぐる空気が変わり始めた。5月1日に広がった市場コメントでは、AnthropicのARRが2026年に入り90億ドルから440億ドル超へ増え、推論インフラのマージンも38%から70%超へ上がったと整理された。これを、NVIDIA $NVDA のGPU需要が弱る話として受け止めると取り違える。むしろ、GPUの上で動くモデル側にも価値が残り始めたというアップデートだ。

2023年以降のAI相場は、価値の回収先がかなり分かりやすかった。訓練も推論も計算資源が足りず、ボトルネックを握ったNVIDIAに資金が集まった。AIモデル企業は成長していても、推論コストが重く、売上の伸びがそのまま利益に変わるとは見られていなかった。

今回の数字が変えているのは、その見方の硬さだ。AnthropicのARRが短期間に4倍超へ伸びるだけなら、需要の強さで説明できる。だが同じ期間に推論マージンが70%超へ改善したなら、トークンを売る事業の単価、稼働率、ソフトウェア最適化が同時に働いている。利用者が実務の中でより多くのトークンを消費し、モデル提供側が1トークンあたりの生産コストを下げる。AIプラットフォームの利益プールは、ハードウェア販売だけでなく、推論の運用そのものへ広がっている。

NVIDIAにとって、これは単純な逆風ではない。推論が儲かるほど、モデル企業はより多くの容量を確保したくなる。ラック、ネットワーク、メモリ、ソフトウェアを含む大規模推論基盤への支出は続く。NVIDIAの強さはGPU単体ではなく、供給制約の中で顧客が最短で大規模推論を立ち上げられるフルスタックにある。

一方で、70%超という数字は会社全体の利益率ではない。訓練費用、研究開発、人材コスト、将来のインフラ契約を含めれば、AIラボのキャッシュ消費はまだ軽くない。だからこの数字だけで、モデル企業が完成した高収益SaaSになったとは言えない。

それでも相場の焦点は変わる。以前は、誰がNVIDIAからGPUを確保できるかが中心だった。これからは、GPUの上で誰が高単価のワークロードを回し、どこまでマージンを守れるかに移っていく。NVIDIAを見るうえでも、GPU出荷だけでは足りない。顧客側のトークン経済がどれだけ厚くなり、その増分をNVIDIAがシステム価格とフルスタック支配でどこまで取り戻すかだ。

我想找到openai和Anthropic比较好的位置买入进去，当我还在持续高频使用它，我愿意持有股票；当我自己都不再使用它，或者它的不可替代性明显下降时，再考虑卖出。

当时我只觉得苹果手机系统好用，但我没有意识到，未来十几年我会一代又一代地更新苹果手机，也没有形成把这种消费习惯转化成投资逻辑的能力，当时也没钱没路子投资苹果股票。

经历过美股好公司长期上涨的逻辑以后，现在手里也有资金，不想错过 AI 这种解放生产力的大机会，我又害怕自己买在情绪最热估值最高的泡沫顶点。
这是目前我和大多数人都有的想法，我们应该如何去判断啊？

quoted_tweet.text:
@dejavucoder Entropy is H(p) = E_p[-log p(x)], your optimal expected code length when you know p. Shannon coding assigns symbol x a length of -log p(x), and on average you can't beat it.

Now suppose you don't know p. You believe it's q, so you build a code where x has length -log q(x). But the data is actually drawn from p, so your expected code length is:

H(p, q) = E_p[-log q(x)]
        = -sum over x of  p(x) * log q(x)
That's cross-entropy: the bits you actually pay using q's codebook on p's data.
KL is just the gap between what you pay and the optimum:
KL(p || q) = H(p, q) - H(p)
           = sum over x of  p(x) * log( p(x) / q(x) )

Or as a one-liner you could literally run:

kl = sum(p[x] * log(p[x] / q[x]) for x in support)

The story in three lines: H(p) is the floor, H(p,q) is what you actually pay with the wrong codebook, KL is the penalty — and it's always ≥ 0 because you can't beat the optimal code.

- Will Brown & Claude Opus 4.7

kept.text:
It is such a fascinating quirk of history--the sort of thing that is not at all structural but is entirely contingent--that OpenAI and Anthropic were both founded during the heyday of "stakeholder capitalism" as a theory that would replace "shareholder capitalism."

The idea was basically that the people who own companies shouldn't be in exclusive control of what companies do, and that we need to bring in the "stakeholders" (which include ~everyone) to govern the corporation.

Like a lot of ideas that came out of the left at the time, it was somehow both remarkably half-baked and also a new way of talking about a very old idea (socialism). 

If they had been founded in any other time period, OpenAI and Anthropic still may have opted for novel corporate governance mechanisms. Their founders would have believed that AGI required a novel institutional form no matter what, I suspect. But the fact that they were contemplating all that when "stakeholder capitalism" was very much in the water among US elites was not at all fated, and probably contaminated the whole "frontier lab as novel institution" project from birth.

Both companies have made significant progress away from the naive model since their founding, to be clear, but the sins of the father are hard to wash away.

Google just completely BETRAYED its principles and people.

In 2018, 4,000 Google employees signed a letter demanding the company to stop helping the Pentagon use AI for drone strikes.

Google listened. They dropped the contract and published a pledge promising they would NEVER use AI for weapons or surveillance.

But Google just signed a $200 million deal to put its most powerful AI on the Pentagon's classified networks for "any lawful purpose."

And the story behind how they got from Point A to Point B is one of the most calculated corporate betrayals in Silicon Valley history.

Here's what happened:

The 2018 protest was over Project Maven, a Pentagon program that used Google's AI to identify targets in drone footage. Employees said "Google should not be in the business of war." 

A dozen engineers quit. Thousands more signed petitions. 

Google folded and let the contract expire.

Then they published their famous AI Principles:

1. No weapons
2. No surveillance
3. No technologies that cause harm

Sundar Pichai personally announced them.

That pledge lasted exactly as long as the contracts didn't matter.

Because here's what Google did quietly over the next 7 years while everyone thought they were the "ethical" AI company:

December 2022: Won a share of the Pentagon's $9 billion Joint Warfighting Cloud contract alongside Amazon, Microsoft, and Oracle.

2024: Deployed Gemini to 3 million Pentagon personnel.

February 2025: Quietly DELETED the weapons and surveillance language from their AI Principles. No announcement or press conference.

April 2026: Signed the classified deal with the Pentagon for "any lawful government purpose."

They didn't have a change of heart.

They had a change of revenue.

And the timing of this deal is where it gets really dark:

600 Google employees, including 20+ directors and VPs from DeepMind, sent a letter to Pichai on Monday begging him not to sign. 

The letter said the only way to guarantee Google's AI wouldn't be used for lethal autonomous weapons or mass surveillance was to reject classified workloads entirely, because once the technology enters air-gapped military networks, nobody at Google can see what happens to it.

But Google literally signed the deal WHILE THE EMPLOYEES SLEPT.

One Google AI researcher told Business Insider: "When I went to bed yesterday, I was hopeful that the employee letter would have an effect. This morning I woke up to the worst-case version of the contract being signed."

The contract says Google has NO right to control or veto how the government uses its AI. The Pentagon can request Google to adjust its AI safety settings. 

And the deal covers classified networks where Google employees cannot monitor what's being done with their own technology.

kept.text:
A lot of people objected to, were even outraged, that restricting the revenue analysis to American software workers.

But let’s think about how much Anthropic revenue could be international. Excluding China and Russia (Claude not available), the US makes up about 20% of global devs. 

But one thing to keep in mind is, to get to $44 billion ARR, the only thing that really matters is devs paying $1k/month. Even Max subscribers don’t move the needle at that scale.

Realistically, devs outside America aren’t expensive enough for this to make financial sense. Median software engineer salary in India (largest international dev market outside US and China) is $30k, is it reasonable believe that Indian firms are casually accepting a 50% cost hike on their software engineers to give them API scale token budgets? Again I believe many, if not most Indian devs are using Claude. But (like myself) my guess is the vast majority are using the much more cost effective subscription and managing limits. 

Same story in most other countries. Median software engineer salary in Europe is $60k, in Japan $50k, in Brazil $30k, in Britain $65k . I’m not saying there is zero API usage in these countries, but I think it’s unrealistic to think these countries have anywhere near the casual API spend that US software engineers have. 

 I think a conservative upper bound on international API revenue is maybe 50%. If we go back to US devs being 20% of the global market, then assuming they have 2X Claude spend intensity seems reasonable. 

So at most, the original analysis has a 2X on the denominator. Doesn’t really fundamentally change anything in the order of magnitudes. To the extent software work is driving the Anthropic ARR, either near majorities of devs must be spending thousands per month, or there must be significant super users who are blowing through tens or even hundreds of thousand per month.

profile_bio.description: Founder @ambient_finance Founding contributor @fogo Ex: Quant

quoted_tweet.text:
I’m really struggling to see how the back of the envelope math on this works out…

There are generously 4 million characterized “software workers” in America. That’s pretty broad and includes a lot of people who aren’t really classical engineers don’t produce that much code. 

That comes out to nearly $1k per month of average Claude spend across every dev in America. Yes, there’s some international usage, but it can’t be that much. Yes there is some non software Cowork usage, but that doesn’t use that many tokens. Yes, some non engineers are using Claude to vibe code, but I really doubt many are spending hundreds per month on. 

Even if we assume 50% of all software workers are using Claude, that comes out to $2k spend per month per Claude user. Thats 10X more than the highest tier Max subscription. So almost all of Anthropics revenue has to be API billing 

So the only explanation is that something like 20%+ of software engineers are not only Claude users but on API billing and regularly spending thousands per month. At $5/m Opus tokens that means the average API user has to be going through something like 25 million tokens per day.

*OR* the other possibility is API revenue is heavily power law dominated. Maybe there’s just something like 100k super users who are making up the majority of the revenue. For that to work the typical super user would have to be spending on the order of $50k/month and guzzling nearly 1 billion tokens per day.

quoted_tweet.text:
THIS GUY ACCIDENTALLY BURNED $6,000 IN CLAUDE USAGE OVERNIGHT WITH ONE COMMAND

he set a /loop command to check his open PRs every 30 minutes the night before and forgot about it

it ran 46 times over 26 hours on opus 4.7. completely unattended

here's how claude billing ACTUALLY works:

every API call sends your ENTIRE conversation history (not just the latest message). 

turn 1 sends a few hundred tokens. turn 46 sends 800,000 tokens

anthropic uses prompt caching to make this cheaper. if your conversation was sent recently it serves from cache at a 12.5x discount

but...cache expires after 5 minutes of inactivity

his /loop was set to 30 minutes. so every single iteration:

> loop fires, history gets cached
> 30 minutes pass, cache expires
> loop fires again, cache is gone
> has to re-cache the entire conversation from scratch at full price
> each iteration also adds its own output so the next re-cache is even bigger

by hour 20 the conversation had grown to 800k tokens. every iteration was paying to re-cache 800k tokens at the expensive write rate. the actual PR check responses were nothing compared to the caching cost (obviously)

anthropic's dashboard has a multi-day reporting lag. he had no idea anything was wrong until the limit email hit

he woke up to a $6,000 bill for a command that was supposed to be a simple PR checker

if you use /loop in claude code, keep the interval under 5 minutes so the cache stays warm. or start a fresh session for each loop

kept.text:
Met an Anthropic alignment lead at a boba spot in SF last Sunday.

He saw the holaOS dashboard on my laptop and stopped mid-order.

"Is that holaOS"

Yeah. Opus 4.7 at the decision layer.

He sat down.

"I run the deception evals team"

"Prediction markets are the hardest eval we have. Counterparty lies on purpose"

What did you find.

"Single-shot Claude gets fooled 41% of the time"

"holaOS mech stacks with cross-verification drop it to 7%"

"That's a paper we haven't published"

I showed him the stack.

https://t.co/7O5SVhJMH7 - mech orchestration + on-chain consensus. MIT. 2,800 stars.

https://t.co/8V2G2m1yIO - autonomous trader. Plugs into holaOS as executor.

https://t.co/OYqwnY2hsM - Claude straight into the CLOB.

One prompt. Every sub-48h Polymarket position runs through a 5-mech verifier loop. Mechs adversarially red-team each other. Trade only if no mech can falsify the thesis.

892 markets in. 14 out.

Opus 4.7 finished in 23 min on a $5 VPS.

"Why falsification not consensus"

Consensus gets manipulated. Falsification doesn't. One break kills the trade.

"The filter"

Zero falsifiers. Edge above 11c. Liquidity above $2.5k. Resolution under 48h. Whale imbalance above 1.7x.

"Why whale imbalance"

Smart money pre-positions. Retail chases. You enter between them.

A fill landed. +$142 on a district market.

"Sizing"

Full Kelly at zero flags. Half Kelly at one. Skip at two.

"Exit"

Any mech flips falsifier → close. Else hold to resolution. Stop 5%.

Sharpe 3.67. 244 trades. 81% WR. $200 → $21,480 in 26 days.

He stared.

"This is the deployment we modeled as unsafe internally. You ran it for a month"

He sipped his boba.

"Refresh the red-team prompts every 5 days. Mechs memorize each other's priors. Staleness kills falsification"

He stood. Left cash.

"If anyone at the lab asks, we never spoke"

We didn't. I just ran the mechs against each other.

He smiled. "Good answer"

Walked out into the fog.

kept.text:
Anthropic in talks with Fractile for SRAM inference chips is the demand-side proof of the thesis I laid out back in January.

This is the news that proves the SRAM inference thesis isn’t a Groq story it’s an architecture.

Fractile, Cerebras, Groq three different vendors, same architectural bet: on-chip SRAM eliminates the HBM shuttle that bottlenecks every autoregressive decode on a GPU. NVIDIA paid ~$20B to bring this capability in-house through the Groq deal. Anthropic is now signaling they want it independent of any single supplier.

The spending math forces the move. When you’re projecting tens of billions a year on chips, you can’t be one-architecture-deep. SRAM-resident inference isn’t an alternative to HBM-based GPUs it’s the second tier of a disaggregated stack. Prefill and long-context attention stay on HBM. Decode moves to SRAM. The labs that internalize this split fastest get the best tokens-per-megawatt.

I called this the design spectrum: SRAM-only → SRAM+DDR → SRAM+HBM. Every frontier model has a different sweet spot on it. Fractile’s value to Anthropic isn’t “cheaper than Nvidia” it’s the architectural freedom to route Claude inference where the physics says it belongs.

Watch the next round of frontier-lab capex announcements. Every one of them is going to have an SRAM line item.

The Memory Wall (Jan 12): https://t.co/dBgM8UE5xp

kept.text:
Five things happened in the last 30 days that should change how every U.S. lawyer thinks about AI.

A bar suspension. Three formal charges. A federal court order. A DOJ intervention. A White House walkback.

Consequences are no longer theoretical.

April 16, 2026. The Nebraska Supreme Court suspended an attorney for AI hallucinations in a divorce brief. The first indefinite license suspension in U.S. history for this.

57 of 63 citations were defective. 20 hallucinations. 3 entirely fabricated cases.

At oral argument, the justices stopped him 37 seconds in.

"The elephant in the room is whether or not you used artificial intelligence. Did you?"

He said no. He later admitted he had.

His client now faces $52,000 in opposing counsel fees.

April 2026. The California State Bar filed formal disciplinary charges against three attorneys for AI-hallucinated citations. Two Notices of Disciplinary Charges. One stipulated discipline. Six misconduct charges in one of the cases.

March 30, 2026. A federal court in Morgan v. V2X, Inc. (D. Colo.) wrote the enterprise-AI requirement directly into a discovery protective order. Consumer-tier AI tools cannot be used to process confidential information. The court acknowledged this would block "most, if not all, mainstream low-to-no-cost AI" for confidential discovery materials.

April 27, 2026. The DOJ filed a Complaint in Intervention to join xAI's lawsuit against Colorado's AI Act. The Colorado AG agreed to halt enforcement until the court rules. The law was set to take effect June 30.

Same week. The White House is crafting an executive action to walk back the Pentagon's "supply chain risk" blacklist of Anthropic. The NSA was using Anthropic's Mythos model the whole time. Political volatility on the vendor side now affects federal practice directly.

A year ago, AI in legal work was a hypothetical risk. Today it's a documented pattern of suspensions, charges, court orders, and regulatory whiplash.

And the profession is reacting to all of it with the wrong question.

The wrong question: "How do I use AI safely for legal work?"
The right question: "Why am I trying to use AI for legal work in the first place?"
Look at where the consequences are landing. Bar suspensions for AI-drafted briefs. Disciplinary charges for AI-fabricated citations. Federal court orders restricting AI for discovery review.

The pattern is consistent: the further AI moves into substantive legal judgment, the higher the malpractice risk, the discipline risk, the protective-order risk.

That is not where the leverage is.

The real use of AI in a law practice is in the non-legal work.

The 60% of your week that doesn't require a law degree.

→ Client intake. AI handles first-call qualification, conflict-check screening, and calendar booking. Zero malpractice risk. 90% time savings.

→ Inbox triage. AI sorts emails, drafts replies in your voice for non-substantive correspondence. You review and send.

→ Engagement letters and welcome emails. Templated structure, AI-personalized to the client. Your judgment governs the scope; AI handles the words.

→ Marketing. AI turns your real cases into LinkedIn posts and newsletter content. Zero hallucination risk — it's writing about facts you control.

→ Scheduling, reminders, time entry, billing. Pure administrative. No citations involved.

And a lot more...

None of this touches legal substance. None of this requires citations. None of this carries the risk that's putting Greg Lake on suspension and three California attorneys in front of the bar.

This is the leverage point most of the profession is missing.

The lawyers fighting publicly over whether AI can write a brief are stuck in the wrong fight.

The lawyers winning quietly are the ones who built agents for everything around the legal work and kept their judgment, their citations, and their bar number out of the AI's hands entirely.

If you're a solo or small firm and you don't know where the line should sit between AI work and your work - that's the diligence call.

And if you want to find out how to use AI effectively into your practice without the risk of hallucinations, how to create workflow automations that actually save time and improve your revenue,,,,let's talk. Link is in the bio.

kept.text:
Whether you love or hate AI, we can all agree the slot machine nature of generative models sucks. Creators need control -- and perhaps the best way to get it is with 3d tools.

But the first time you open blender, it’s overwhelming. Like staring at a 747 cockpit with a dizzying number of buttons. And even when you become a seasoned pilot -- you absolutely wish you had autopilot for the mundane bits.

Connectors for AI models like claude were supposed to be the best of both worlds -- like having an expert python coder sitting next to you, driving blender through its API while you get complete visibility and control.

Unlike generative image/video models, you’re not surrendering to output you can’t precisely modify, inspect, or understand. You’re directing it to use blender for you. Truly the best of both worlds.

But alas the announcement resulted in instant backlash, and the patronage got pulled by Friday…

The connector still ships -- that part survives. What didn’t survive though was the brand association between the Blender Foundation and Anthropic.

The trust deficit runs so deep that even the most thoughtful, opt in, inspectable AI integration can’t be publicly tied to an AI lab.

Disincentivizing even beneficial applications to 3d creation feels like a real loss -- but gosh, i can imagine the backlash has been overwhelming for the blender team.

Some tell me this is oil and water -- never the twain shall meet. Perhaps or maybe it’s just a matter of time. One thing I’m certain of is that before it gets better, it’ll probably get worse.

Andrew Chen just called PM the most important role in tech again.

His argument in one line: when anyone can build, the person who decides WHAT to build becomes the bottleneck.

Boris Cherny’s Claude Code team at Anthropic shows what this looks like in practice. They ship hundreds of prototypes before committing to a feature. Boris personally runs 5 parallel Claude instances and ships 20-30 PRs a day. The team built Cowork, a full product for non-engineers, in about 10 days. The cost of building dropped through the floor. The cost of picking the right thing didn’t.

Anthropic’s PMs don’t even write traditional PRDs anymore. They review working software at 9am, kill 80% of it by noon, ship the rest by end of week. Pattern-matching across user research and technical feasibility while staring at a working prototype.

This is why AI PM offers at OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google DeepMind now run past $1M total comp. Anyone can build. Almost nobody can look at 15 working prototypes and call the 3 worth scaling. <AI 조달 기준 재편과 ‘Mythos’ 대응 방향에 대한 정책적 고민>

최근 백악관이 Anthropic의 최신 모델 ‘Mythos’에 대해 연방기관 도입을 재검토하고 있다는 보도가 있었습니다. 이는 기존 공급망 리스크 평가를 일정 부분 완화하거나 우회하는 방식으로, 연방정부가 최신 프론티어 AI 모델을 다시 활용할 수 있도록 조달 기준을 조정하려는 움직임으로 해석됩니다.

이번 사안은 특정 기업이나 개별 모델의 문제가 아니라, AI 시대에 국가가 어떤 기준과 원칙으로 기술을 도입·활용할 것인가에 대한 보다 본질적인 질문을 제기합니다. 특히 미국 국방부와의 협력 범위를 둘러싼 이견—일부 AI 기업은 대량 감시 및 완전자율무기 활용을 제한하는 원칙을 유지하는 반면, 정부는 ‘합법적 목적’ 범위를 폭넓게 해석하려는 입장—은 기술 윤리와 국가안보 운용 간 긴장을 상징적으로 보여주는 사례입니다.

이번 논의에서 주목해야 할 지점은 크게 두 가지입니다.

첫째, 프론티어 모델 확보 경쟁의 본격화입니다.
연방기관이 최신 AI 모델 도입을 적극 검토하고 있다는 점은, 최고 수준의 모델 접근성이 곧 국가 경쟁력으로 직결된다는 인식이 확산되고 있음을 의미합니다. 이는 단순한 기술 도입을 넘어 정책 결정, 정보 분석, 국방, 과학 연구 등 국가 기능 전반에 AI가 구조적으로 통합되고 있음을 시사합니다.

둘째, 조달 기준의 유연화 흐름입니다.
안전 원칙을 유지하면서도 실제 운용 요구에 맞춰 기준을 조정하려는 시도는 향후 글로벌 AI 거버넌스의 중요한 방향성을 제시할 수 있습니다. 규제 중심과 완전 자율 사이에서, ‘조건부 신뢰 기반 활용’이라는 현실적 접근이 부상하고 있습니다.

이러한 변화는 우리에게도 중요한 정책적 시사점을 제공합니다.

우선, 특정 모델 대응을 넘어 국가 차원의 AI 조달·활용 프레임워크 정립이 필요합니다. 공공부문 AI 도입 시 ▲보안성 ▲데이터 주권 ▲모델 투명성 ▲윤리 기준 준수 등을 종합적으로 평가하는 체계를 구축해야 합니다.

또한, 자체 프론티어 모델 역량 확보 역시 병행되어야 합니다. 단순한 기술 도입을 넘어, 필요 시 독자적 개발과 운용이 가능한 역량을 확보해야 전략적 자율성을 담보할 수 있습니다. 이러한 측면에서 현재 추진 중인 ‘독자 AI 파운데이션 모델(독파모)’ 사업은 중요한 기반이 될 것입니다.

아울러, 정부와 AI 기업 간 협력 구조의 재정립도 필요합니다. 기업의 윤리 원칙과 국가의 안보 요구가 충돌할 가능성에 대비하여, 사전에 협의 가능한 기준과 적용 범위를 명확히 하고 이를 제도화하는 노력이 요구됩니다.

결국 ‘Mythos’ 사례는 개별 기술 이슈를 넘어, AI 시대 국가가 어떠한 원칙과 전략으로 기술을 수용하고 활용할 것인가를 가늠하는 하나의 시험대라고 볼 수 있습니다. 동시에, 이를 둘러싼 논의를 본격화하는 중요한 계기가 되고 있습니다.

그동안 ‘Mythos’ 대응과 관련하여 정부 주관 실무 회의가 총 4차례 진행되었습니다. 향후 보다 면밀한 검토와 체계적인 대응을 통해 불확실성을 해소하고, 대한민국이 나아가야 할 방향을 명확히 정립해 나가겠습니다.

앞으로도 ‘어떤 기준과 철학으로 AI를 활용할 것인가’라는 근본적 질문을 중심에 두고, 보다 책임 있고 전략적인 정책을 추진해 나가겠습니다.

tweet.text:
AN ANTHROPIC ENGINEER JUST REVEALED THAT MOST PEOPLE ARE USING MCP WRONG.

And the part they are missing is worth more than everything they are currently using it for.

Most builders know MCP as a tool-calling protocol.

Drop in a server. Call a function. Get a result.

That is 20% of what it actually does.

Here are the 5 primitives most people have never heard of:

PROMPTS — Predefined templates that inject specific text directly into the context window. The prompt completion feature alone is one of the most powerful underutilized capabilities in the entire protocol.

RESOURCES — Raw data exposure for your application. Database schemas. Live data feeds. Anything that enables richer retrieval-augmented generation without manual piping.

TOOLS — The part everyone knows. Standard actions invoked by the model.

SAMPLING — The one that changes everything. Servers can request completions from the CLIENT'S configured model. Centralized control over privacy, cost, and security without breaking the agent loop.

ROOTS — Servers can query what workspace or project context the client is currently in. VS Code directories. Open files. Live project state.

And that is just where MCP is today.

Here is where it is going.

OAuth 2.1 authorization so MCP servers can bind to your enterprise identity provider. Azure AD. Okta. Real security at scale.

Streamable HTTP so MCP servers function like standard APIs while still supporting complex streaming interactions.

An official registry where you can discover and publish servers publicly.

Ruby SDK from Shopify. Go SDK from the Google Go team.

MCP is not a tool-calling protocol.

It is becoming the operating layer for every AI agent that touches real enterprise infrastructure.

The builders who understand all 5 primitives right now are building systems that the people who only know tool-calling cannot replicate.

Bookmark this.

Follow @cyrilXBT for every MCP development the moment it drops.
